title: Garlic honey for colds
subtitle: Raw garlic steeped in honey, an old kitchen remedy
excerpt: Crushed garlic cloves left to steep in raw honey for 24 hours. A first-response kitchen tradition taken at the start of a cold. Pungent, sharp, and soothed by the honey.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), garlic entry. Garlic as an antimicrobial, chest-clearing, immune-stimulating herb. King's American Dispensatory (Felter and Lloyd, 1898), garlic as an antiseptic and cold remedy. WHO Monograph on Allium sativum (1999), evidence review of allicin as the key antimicrobial constituent.

content.text: Crushed garlic left to macerate in raw honey for 24 hours. The honey draws out the

and other sulphur compounds from the garlic. The honey softens the harshness and acts as a mild preservative. Taken by the teaspoon at the start of a cold, or stirred into a mug of hot water with lemon. A kitchen tradition long made for the head cold and the tickly chest. Pungent, sharp, and easy to spot on the breath of whoever made it.

attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a medical professional for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Anticoagulants: garlic can thin the blood. Cooking doses (one to two cloves a day) are unlikely to matter. Daily concentrated garlic preparations are best discussed with the prescriber if you take warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel, or other blood thinners.

Children under two: do not give honey to children under two. Honey can carry botulism spores. For children two and over, half a teaspoon is within the traditional range.

Pregnancy: cooking amounts of garlic in pregnancy are well established as safe. Therapeutic daily doses are less well studied. One or two teaspoons a day sit close to a cooking range, but discuss with your midwife if you use it regularly.

Breath and body odour: the garlic comes out partly through the lungs and skin. Expected and temporary.
attrs.title: Medical notes

items.name: Garlic
items.prepNote: crushed or coarsely chopped, the crushing releases the allicin

items.name: Honey
items.prepNote: raw, unpasteurised honey if possible, it retains its own antimicrobial compounds

content.text: Peel {{garlic}} and crush or coarsely chop each clove, a flat blade pressed down with the heel of the hand, or a few pulses in a pestle and mortar. The goal is to break the cell walls and release the allicin; slicing thinly achieves the same effect. Place the crushed garlic directly into the sterilised jar.

Pour {{honey}} of honey over the garlic. Stir to combine, the honey will be thick and the garlic will settle on the sides. Seal the jar. Within a few hours the garlic will begin to release liquid into the honey, making it more pourable. Leave at room temperature for at least 24 hours, ideally 48, before using. Turn the jar upside down and back periodically to distribute the garlic through the honey.

Adults: 1 teaspoon (5 ml) of the garlic honey, taken neat or stirred into a small cup of warm water with a squeeze of lemon, up to three times a day at the onset of a cold. Continue for three to five days. The garlic cloves themselves can also be eaten, one or two crushed cloves a day is within the traditional therapeutic range. If the cold is not improving after five days, see your doctor.

Keeps at room temperature in a sealed jar for 4 weeks; refrigerate for up to 3 months. The honey will crystallise in the fridge, gentle warming in a bowl of warm water returns it to a pourable consistency.
When NOT to use this remedy

Do not give to children under two. Talk to the prescriber if you take blood thinners. Seek medical care if cold symptoms come with a high fever, trouble breathing, chest pain, or a sharp worsening after early improvement.
